coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of biomass waste derived graphene quantum dots with high ion detection ability
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yingjuan
Luo, Meng
Yang, Rong
Yao, Siyu
Zhang, Qianwei
Yu, Wei
Feng, Zufei
Yan, Yinglin
Xu, Yunhua
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hold extensive application prospects in ion detection owing to their high sensitivity, excellent water solubility, strong fluorescence stability and great biocompatibility. In here, GQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method, utilizing biomass waste bean dregs and coffee grounds as raw materials, respectively. The optimum conditions for the preparation of GQDs were explored, and their structure, morphology and optical properties were characterized. The results show that the particle size of GQDs-B derived from bean dregs is about 1.6 nm and exhibits a more uniform distribution than that of GQDs-C obtained from coffee grounds. Additionally, the GQDs-B displays good aqueous solubility, higher degree of graphitization and abundant surface functional groups, with the fluorescence quantum yield of 21.32 %. When GQDs-B was employed as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ and Ce4+, a favorable linear relationship was observed between fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Fe3+ and Ce4+, with the fitting factor is 0.993 and 0.911, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.0019 mM and 0.0025 mM, respectively. Moreover, the Ce4+@GQDs-B fluorescence probe was fabricated, and the fitting factor between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of PO4 3- is 0.971. This study provides a green and environmentally friendly approach for the preparation of high-performance GQDs, and the synthesized GQDs demonstrate great potential for sensing application in water environment, thereby contributing to the development of advanced materials for environmental monitoring and analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis
Fluorescent probe
Ion detection
Water environment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic demonstration of the synthesis process of GQDs.

The effect of different experimental parameters on the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized GQDs-B, (a) the proportion of raw materials, (b) the synthesis temperature, and (c) the synthesis duration.
The effects of different factors on the fluorescence stability of the synthesized GQDs-B, (a, d) pH, (b, e) ion concentration, and (c, f) placement duration.
TEM images (inset: HRTEM images) of (a) GQDs-B, and (e) GQDs-C. Particle size distribution of (b) GQDs-B, and (f) GQDs-C. AFM images of (c, d) GQDs-B, and (g, h) GQDs-C.
(a) Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra of GQDs-B and GQDs-C, displaying strong absorption in the UV range. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra for (b) GQDs-B, and (c) GQDs-C.
(a) Raman, and (b) FTIR spectrum of GQDs-B and GQDs-C.
High-resolution XPS of (a, d) C 1s, (b, e) O 1s, and (c, f) N 1s for GQDs-B and GQDs-C.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-B solution in the existence of various metal ions at 380 nm excitation. (b) Column graph to compare the emission intensity of GQDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions.

Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-B with (a) Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.6 mM, (b) Ce4+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.6 mM. (c) Fluorescence spectra of Ce4+@GQDs-B with PO4 3− concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.4 mM. Linear regression plot of GQDs-B fluorescence intensity with (d) c(Fe3+) and (e) c(Ce4+). (f) Linear regression plot of Ce4+@GQDs-B fluorescence intensity with c(PO4 3−).

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of biomass waste derived graphene quantum dots with high ion detection ability

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology, Institute of Chemical Power Sources, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an 710048, Shaanxi, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hold extensive application prospects in ion detection owing to their high sensitivity, excellent water solubility, strong fluorescence stability and great biocompatibility. In here, GQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method, utilizing biomass waste bean dregs and coffee grounds as raw materials, respectively. The optimum conditions for the preparation of GQDs were explored, and their structure, morphology and optical properties were characterized. The results show that the particle size of GQDs-B derived from bean dregs is about 1.6 nm and exhibits a more uniform distribution than that of GQDs-C obtained from coffee grounds. Additionally, the GQDs-B displays good aqueous solubility, higher degree of graphitization and abundant surface functional groups, with the fluorescence quantum yield of 21.32 %. When GQDs-B was employed as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ and Ce4+, a favorable linear relationship was observed between fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Fe3+ and Ce4+, with the fitting factor is 0.993 and 0.911, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.0019 mM and 0.0025 mM, respectively. Moreover, the Ce4+@GQDs-B fluorescence probe was fabricated, and the fitting factor between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of PO4 3- is 0.971. This study provides a green and environmentally friendly approach for the preparation of high-performance GQDs, and the synthesized GQDs demonstrate great potential for sensing application in water environment, thereby contributing to the development of advanced materials for environmental monitoring and analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs has the advantages of strong fluorescence stability [1,2], low toxicity [3], high water solubility [4] and excellent optical and electronic properties [5,6]. It can be used in the fields of fluorescence sensors [7–10], biological imaging [11], photocatalysis [12], solar cells [13], light-emitting diodes [14], drug delivery [15,16] and lithium‑sulfur batteries [17,18]. Due to the quantum confinement effect, GQDs exhibits different electronic and optical properties from graphene, such as fluorescence emission [19], adjustable band gap and so on [20]. These characteristics rely heavily on the size and edge structure of GQDs and provide the possibility for the development of new electronic devices and biomarkers.

At present, many strategies have been developed for the synthesis of GQDs, including top-down approaches starting with graphene or CNT, as well as bottom-up approaches based on small molecules as precursors [21,22]. However, many of these strategies rely on costly, non-renewable precursors or require toxic and dangerous chemical reagents, thereby limiting their sustainable development and large-scale application [23,24]. Recently, green hydrothermal synthesis has been favored by people due to its economic feasibility, operational simplicity, environmental compatibility, and complete equipment functions [25]. Biomass, owing to its abundant sources, low cost and natural carbon-rich composition is considered a safe, sustainable and eco-friendly alternative source of organic carbon [26]. Many studies have demonstrated the preparation of GQDs from biomass wastes such as orange peel [27], waste tea [28], withania somnifera leaves [29] and so on. Among these, bean dregs and spent coffee grounds are particularly promising. Bean dregs are rich in protein and cellulose, providing abundant carbon and nitrogen sources, while coffee grounds contain caffeine, lignin and polyphenols, offering natural nitrogen/oxygen functional groups [30]. Both of them can directly form heteroatom-containing GQDs via hydrothermal reaction, reducing subsequent functionalization steps. In addition, both bean dregs and coffee grounds are common household biomass wastes with negligible cost. There is no need for harsh acid or oxidizing pretreatments, thereby minimizing waste disposal issues and aligning well with the principle of green chemistry.

Iron is a common metal contaminant found in both industrial wastewater and drinking water [31]. Currently, the concentration of Fe3+ has emerged as a crucial parameter for evaluating the quality of the water environment [32,33]. Due to the extensive open-pit mining of rare earth mines, the rare earth element cerium has been introduced into the soil and water systems. Cerium can accumulate in the human body, potentially leading to the formation of lung and liver thrombosis [34]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to explore rapid monitoring methods for cerium. Meanwhile, the phosphate ion (PO4 3-), as a key component of the food chain of aquatic microorganisms, leads to the consumption of dissolved oxygen and the decay and death of plants and aquatic organisms in the event of eutrophication [35,36]. To some extent, PO4 3- can serve as an effective indicator or tracker of organic pollution in water [37], and its detection is of utmost significance for preventing and controlling the occurrence of eutrophication [38].

However, the existing ion detection methods usually suffer from drawbacks such as low accuracy, poor qualitative effect, low selectivity, harsh experimental conditions and so on [39]. In contrast, GQDs possess the advantages of cost-effectiveness, non-toxicity, simple operation, high sensitivity, excellent biocompatibility [25], water solubility and stable fluorescence characteristics, making it a suitable candidate for the design of optical detection system for fluorescent nanomaterials [40]. Therefore, it holds great promise and a broad development vista to rationally design an ion detection probe based on the fluorescence properties of GQDs and apply it to the detection of ions in the water environment, which is of great importance for safeguarding water quality and ecological balance.

In this study, a hydrothermal synthesis of GQDs, employing biomass waste bean dregs and coffee grounds as carbon sources respectively, and deionized water (DI) as solvents is proposed without any oxidation, reduction or passivator. The GQDs-B synthesized from bean dregs shows spherical morphology, possessing an average particle size of 1.63 nm and hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Meanwhile, it is very suitable for sensing applications because of its excellent optical properties. Beyond its application in the detection of Fe3+ and Ce4+, a new probe system is designed in this work, which is realized by the Ce4+@GQDs-B fluorescence recovery phenomenon induced by PO4 3-. The fitting factor between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of PO4 3- is 0.971, indicating its potential as a sensor to detect PO4 3- in water, which has potential application value in the field of water environment monitoring.

Bean dregs and coffee grounds were collected from nearby breakfast shop and coffee shop respectively. Salts including NaCl, KCl, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, ZnCl2, AlCl3, AgNO3, CaCO3, CuSO4·5H2O, CeS2O8·4H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O and CoCl2·6H2O were obtained from commercial suppliers and were used directly without purification. The water filter membrane (0.22 μm) was provided by Shanghai Xinya Purification Device Factory, and the Molecular Weight Cut Off (MWCO) dialysis bag was used to purify GQDs (Foshan Jielup Machinery Technology Co., Ltd.). All solutions use DI as solvent, and the pure water machine was provided by Sichuan Yupu Ultra Pure Technology Co., Ltd.

Bean dregs and coffee grounds were employed as precursors respectively, and GQDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. Washed bean dregs and coffee grounds thoroughly in DI to remove soluble impurities, then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h after natural air drying for 24 h to remove residual moisture, and ground into fine powder (< 100 mm) using a commercial grinder. 1 g dried bean dregs powder and coffee grounds powder were dissolved in 20 mL DI respectively, and then the mixture were treated by ultrasonic for 1 h. After that, the above mixture were transferred into a 50 mL reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, and reacted hydrothermally at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution were filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Finally, the GQDs solution were further purified by dialysis bag with the molecular interception of MWCO = 3000 for 48 h, and then stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C. The GQDs prepared with bean dregs and coffee grounds as the precursor is marked as GQDs-B and GQDs-C in turn.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) reflects the efficiency of fluorescence emission and is a key indicator for evaluating the optical properties of GQDs, which is generally measured by the reference method. The QY of the prepared GQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (QS, QY = 54.6 %) as a reference. The QY of the prepared GQDs is calculated according to the following formula:
(1)
QY GQDs
=
QY QS
K GQDs
K QS
η GQDs
η QS
where QYGQDs and QYQS mean the fluorescence quantum yields of the analyte and the reference object, respectively. KGQDs and KQS represent the slope of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance curves of GQDs and quinine sulfate; η is the refractive index of the solvent.

The structure, morphology and properties of the prepared GQDs were characterized by a series of analytical and testing techniques. The colloidal stability was characterized by Zeta potential test. The shape, size, microstructure, the thickness and layers of the samples were evaluated by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The structural configuration and surface functional groups of GQDs were studied by Raman spectroscopy and FTIR-8900 infrared spectrometer. The surface chemistry and chemical bonding environments were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The excitation and emission spectra were recorded by Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absorption spectrum was recorded by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer, and the GQDs were qualitatively analyzed.

Bright blue luminescent GQDs with high quality were prepared using green biomass waste bean dregs and coffee grounds as raw materials and DI as solvent, respectively. The synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 .

GQDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The conversion of bean dregs and coffee grounds to GQDs is a multi-stage catalytic process, which is first the separation of crystalline cellulose and amorphous cellulose, then the conversion of crystalline cellulose into irregular carbon nanocrystals, and then the development of aromatic carbon [41]. However, the hydrothermal synthesis method successfully completed all the reactions in one step, and the crystalline cellulose in the precursor has a great ability to convert into graphite carbon. High temperature and high pressure, as well as the formation of hydroxyl ions are the reasons for graphitization [42].

Taking the preparation of GQDs-B as an example, the effects of ratio of raw material, and temperature and duration of hydrothermal synthesis on the photoluminescence intensity of GQDs-B were studied. Firstly, 1 g bean dregs were added to 10 mL, 15 mL, 20 mL, and 25 mL DI respectively, and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h to synthesize GQDs-B. The fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrometer. In Fig. 2(a), the results show that the fluorescence intensity of the series GQDs-B solution increases gradually when the volume of DI increases from 10 mL to 20 mL. The reason is that bean dreg powders can’t be completely dissolved in solvents when the content of DI is too low, and the excessive concentration of GQDs formed by carbonization leads to mutual aggregation, which leads to the lower fluorescence intensity of GQDs solution. When the dosage of DI reaches 25 mL, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs solution decreases, which may be due to the low concentration of GQDs-B solution.

Further, the effect of hydrothermal temperatures on the fluorescence properties of GQDs is explored. The fluorescence spectrum of GQDs solution reacted at different hydrothermal temperatures for 12 h is shown in Fig. 2(b). When the hydrothermal temperature increases from 140 °C to 180 °C, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs increases gradually, and the fluorescence intensity of GQDs reaches the peak at 180 °C, and then decreases with the increase of reaction temperature.

The fluorescence spectra of GQDs prepared under different hydrothermal duration is displayed in Fig. 2(c). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the prepared GQDs increases gradually with the increase of reaction time, and reaches the peak at 12 h. This is because the reaction is more sufficient with the increase of reaction time. Then the fluorescence intensity decreases while the reaction time increases continuously, which is due to the excessive reaction time and the agglomeration of GQDs to a certain extent, which leads to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. Therefore, 1 g bean dregs were dissolved in 20 mL DI to prepare precursor solution, and hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 12 h is used as the experimental parameter for the preparation of GQDs.

The stability of fluorescent nanomaterials is a crucial property for the advancement of sensors, making it imperative to investigate the factors that influence the fluorescence stability of GQDs. By employing GQDs-B, synthesized with the optimal experimental parameters, we aim to examine the impact of varying pH, ionic solution, and room temperature storage conditions on the fluorescence stability of GQDs-B.

NaOH and HCl solutions were employed to adjust and configure GQDs-B solutions with pH of 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10 and 12. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs-B under different pH conditions were quantified by fluorescence spectrometer. The outcomes are depicted in Fig. 3 (a, d). It is evident that the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs-B solution reaches the maximum when pH is 7. Moreover, the fluorescence emission intensity of GQDs-B under alkaline condition is lower than that in acidic condition. This phenomenon could be attributed to the protonation of basic sites on the surface of GQDs-B, leading to a change in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-B.

GQDs solution with varying concentrations of NaCl (0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 mol/L) was prepared, and the fluorescence intensities of the GQDs at each ion concentration were measured by fluorescence spectrometer. The results are shown in Fig. 3(b, e). After the GQDs-B solution was deposited at room temperature for 3000 s, it is observed that the fluorescence intensities are not significantly affected, indicating that the prepared GQDs-B exhibit good fluorescence stability.

When the GQDs-B solution was placed at room temperature for duration of 0, 600, 1200, 1800, 2400, and 3000 s, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs-B were quantified by fluorescence spectrometer. Observations from Fig. 3(c, f) reveal that the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs-B solution remains relatively stable. This indicates that GQDs-B possesses good fluorescence stability and does not undergo aggregation, even in the condition of high ionic strength.

Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY values of GQDs-B and GQDs-C were calculated by to be 21.32 % and 19.76 %, respectively, based on the comparative method. The comparative analysis of GQDs synthesized from various biomass precursors is presented in Table S1.

In order to verify the stability of the prepared GQDs in water, we carried out Zeta potential test. The results are shown in Fig. S1. GQDs-B exhibited a Zeta potential of -11.36 mV, which is slightly more negative than that of GQDs-C (-8.99 mV) in aqueous medium. These negative values indicate that both types of GQDs possess negatively charge surface, which contribute to their colloidal stability in water through electrostatic repulsion [26]. Such repulsive Coulomb forces, primarily arising from carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of GQDs, effectively inhibit nanoparticle agglomeration through electrostatic repulsion, even without the aid of surface-stabilizing agents [43]. For thermal stability evaluation, we placed the prepared GQDs solution at different temperatures (4 °C, 25 °C, and 80 °C). After standing at room temperature (25 °C) for 15 days, no visible precipitation or color change occurred in the GQDs solution (Fig. S2(a, d)), indicating that it has long-term water stability. Meanwhile, the prepared GQDs were subjected to short-term high-temperature treatment. The GQDs solution was placed in a water bath at 80 °C for 24 h, respectively. No precipitation or agglomeration was observed (Fig. S2(c, f)), indicating that its short-term heat resistance is satisfactory. In addition, the GQDs solution stored at 4 °C for 15 days did not crystallize or aggregate, as shown in Fig. S2(b, e), indicating that it is suitable for cryopreservation.

The morphology, particle size and dispersion of GQDs-B and GQDs-C prepared under optimized conditions were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 4(a) presents the TEM image of GQDs-B, revealing that the synthesized GQDs-B particles are spherical and uniformly dispersed without agglomeration. In contrast, Fig. 4(e) illustrates that GQDs-C has a notably inferior dispersion, with visible agglomeration. The statistical analysis of the particle sizes of GQDs-B and GQDs-C were carried out by Nano measurer software, with the outcomes depicted in Fig. 4(b, f). GQDs-B shows the range of particle size distribution of 0.38-3.74 nm, with an average particle size is 1.63 nm. GQDs-C has a larger particle size range from 1.92 nm to 9.84 nm, with an average particle size of 4.81 nm. The microstructure of GQDs-B and GQDs-C were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), As shown in the illustration of the inset in Fig. 4(a, e), clear lattice fringes were observed, with interplanar spacings of 0.274 nm for GQDs-B and 0.277 nm for GQDs-C, respectively, which is displaying well-defined lattice fringes that indicate a high degree of crystallinity [25]. Additionally, the diffusion ring in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern reveals the polycrystalline characteristics of GQDs-B (Fig. S3). In addition, the longitudinal height of the prepared GQDs was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The results show that the thickness of GQDs-B was about 0.1-0.6 nm (Fig. 4(c, d)), indicating that the thickness of GQDs-B is 1-2 layers. The thickness of GQDs-C is about 0.1-1.0 nm (Fig. 4(g, h)), suggesting that the thickness is 1-3 layers. These findings confirm that the synthesized GQDs exhibit structural features consistent with typical graphene quantum dots prepared via hydrothermal methods [44].

The optical properties of GQDs were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Fig. 5(a) shows that GQDs-B has two obvious UV–vis absorption peaks compared to GQDs-C. The typical absorption peak at 230 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition in the aromatic sp2 domain, and the absorption peak at 273 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition between the oxygen-containing group and the sp2 domain [45]. According to the test results of Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 6(a)), GQDs-B has a higher degree of graphitization, and high graphitization can enhance the π-π* electron transition, resulting in a sharper and stronger absorption peak of UV–vis at 273 nm. In addition, the absorption peak width gradually narrowed, indicating that the particle size of GQD became more and more uniform [46]. The average particle size of GQDs-B is smaller than that of GQDs-C, and the quantum confinement effect is significant, so the absorption peak intensity is higher. When compared to GQDs-C, GQDs-B demonstrates stronger absorption, thereby affirming its superior optical properties.

The optical properties of GQDs are further investigated by fluorescence spectra. Fig. 5(b, c) illustrates the effect of different wavelength on the fluorescence spectra of GQDs. It is evident that the fluorescence emission peaks of GQDs-B and GQDs-C shift in response to changes in excitation wavelength, showing a good excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs increases as the excitation wavelength increases, and the strongest peak appears when 380 nm is employed as the excitation wavelength. The GQDs synthesized in this work shows photoluminescence based on excitation, as the unique structural groups lead to multiple emission states, revealing obvious and attractive optical properties.

Raman spectroscopy was used to evaluate the degree of graphitization and the structural characteristics of the biomass-derived GQDs, as shown in Fig. 6(a). The Raman spectra of GQDs-B and GQDs-C shows the prominent D band in the range of 1338–1409 cm-1, associated with the structural defect and disordered sp3 carbon. The G band, appearing at 1557–1591 cm-1 is attributed to the in-plane vibration mode of graphitized sp2 carbon. The ID/IG values of GQDs-B and GQDs-C are 0.80 and 0.84, respectively, suggesting that GQDs-B exhibits a higher degree of graphitization and a more conjugated graphene-like structure compared to GQDs-C [47].

The structure and surface functional groups of the synthesized GQDs were studied by infrared spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 6(b) displays that the prepared GQDs surface is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. The absorption peak observed at 2925 cm-1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CH bond in methylene groups, while the peak at 1049 cm-1 belongs to the characteristic vibration of CO group. The absorption peak at 1637 cm-1 is the tensile vibration of CO, the peak at 1458 cm-1 represents the asymmetric stretching vibration peak of CN, and the peak at 1402 cm-1 is associated with the bending vibration mode of -CH2. Lastly, the absorption band at 3417 cm-1 belongs to the characteristic vibration of OH bond and NH bond [48]. The presence of the aforementioned absorption peaks indicates that GQDs is successfully synthesized and is rich in hydrophilic groups, including -OH and -COOH. These functional groups not only facilitate surface modification of the synthesized GQDs, but also impart hydrophilicity and stability, ensuring them stable and easy to diverge in aqueous environments.

In addition, the elemental composition and surface chemical state of the prepared GQDs were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in the full XPS spectra (Fig. S5), the major peaks located at 285.1, 399.5 and 532.9 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively, indicating the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements in both GQDs-B and GQDs-C. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of GQDs-B and GQDs-C are fitted into six peaks shown in Fig. 7 (a, d), corresponding to sp2 CC (284.3 eV), sp3 CC (285 eV), CN (285.9 eV), CO (286.4 eV), carbonyl CO (287.6 eV), and O-C=O (288.5 eV) in the carboxyl group. This revealed that the carbon atoms had been functionalized with oxygen and possessed different chemical binding states [44]. The N 1 s spectra of GQDs-B and GQDs-C can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 399.5, 400.1, 401.3 eV, as shown in Fig. 7(b, e), which are attributed to NH bond, pyrrole-N and graphite-N, respectively. It is shown that a part of the nitrogen element in the biomass raw material is successfully doped into the carbon matrix of the GQDs. Furthermore, the O 1 s spectra reveal peaks at 532.4 eV and 531.1 eV, as shown in Fig. 7(c, f), corresponding to carbonyl (C=O) and quinone-like oxygen species, respectively. These observations are consistent with the FTIR results, further confirming the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the prepared GQDs surfaces, which may contribute to their fluorescence properties and ion detection performance.

Owing to their abundant surface functionalization and excellent optical properties, these GQDs are suitable for ion detection and sensitive sensors. Based on the obvious and attractive morphology and optical properties, GQDs-B was selected to explore the application prospect of GQDs in ion detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GQDs in ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned earlier, the synthesized GQDs exhibit excellent optical properties, uniform size and morphology, and are rich in hydrophilic groups. Therefore, the prepared GQDs can serve as a novel optical sensing probe for ion detection. By detecting the quenching behavior of the synthesized GQDs-B, its ability to detect ions and its practical application value were explored.

A variety of metal ions, including Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ag+ and Ce4+, were added into the GQDs-B solution. As shown in Fig. 8 (a, b), the majority of these metal ions preserve the fluorescence stability of GQDs-B. However, when Fe3+ and Ce4+ are introduced, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-B decreases obviously, indicating that GQDs-B has a strong selectivity for the detection of Fe3+ and Ce4+. To delve deeper into the detection capabilities of GQDs for Fe3+ and Ce4+, GQDs-B solutions with Fe3+ concentrations of 0-1.6 mM and Ce4+ concentrations of 0-2.0 mM were prepared, and the fluorescence intensity of these solutions was then measured by fluorescence spectrometer. The results, as shown in Fig. 9 (a, b), indicate a progressive decrease in fluorescence intensity corresponding to the increase of Fe3+ and Ce4+ concentrations. Fig. 9(d, e) illustrate the linear regression plot of fluorescence intensity with ion concentrations, it can be seen that there is a strong linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and c(Fe3+), c(Ce4+), with the fitting degree factors of 0.993 and 0.911 respectively. These results demonstrate that the prepared GQDs-B can serve as a highly sensitivity and selective probe for Fe3+ and Ce4+ detection. Using the formula
LOD =
3 σ
K
, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined, where K is the slop of the linear regression curve and σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements. In this study, the prepared GQDs-B was used as a detection probe for Fe3+ and Ce4+, and the LOD was measured to be 0.0019 mM and 0.0025 mM, respectively.
To better contextualize the performance of GQDs-B, we have provided a comparison in Table S2, which includes parameters LOD and selectivity for GQDs-based sensors in previously published studies.

In addition, when Na2HPO4·12H2O was added to the solution containing varied concentrations of Ce4+@GQDs-B, the fluorescence intensity of Ce4+@GQDs-B recovered gradually following the introduction of PO4 3-. As depicted in Fig. 9(c, f), at a concentration of 2 mM Ce4+ in Ce4+@GQDs-B, the photoluminescence intensity rises with the increase of PO4 3- concentration from 0 mM to 1.4 mM. This may be due to the strong affinity between PO4 3- and Ce4+, which destroys the aggregation of Ce4+@GQDs-B, thereby restoring the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. The linear regression diagram of fluorescence intensity and c (PO4 3-) reveals a good linear correlation in the range of 0-1.4 mM, and the fitting degree factor is 0.971. Consequently, a Ce4+@GQDs-B probe system can be constructed for selective detection of PO4 3-.

The utilization of zero-cost biomass precursors, coupled with environmentally friendly benign and economical solvents, and a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis process, collectively exemplifies a sustainable strategy for GQDs fabrication. Moreover, the functional performance of GQDs-B in ion detection highlights its potential applicability in environmental monitoring and sensing technologies.

GQDs-B and GQDs-C were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using waste biomass-bean dregs and spent coffee grounds as carbon precursors, with the synthesis conditions meticulously optimized. The QY values of GQDs-B and GQDs-C are determined to be 21.32 % and 19.76 %, respectively. Among the two, GQDs-B derived from bean dregs exhibited superior optical and structural properties. The average particle diameter of GQDs-B is 1.63 nm with excellent dispersion. AFM images reveal a thickness range of 0.1–0.6 nm. UV–vis spectroscopy shows a significant absorption peak attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond in the structure of GQDs-B. Raman spectrum further signifies a higher degree of graphitization of GQDs-B. FTIR analysis reveals the presence of functional groups such as OH, CC, and CO which is consistent with XPS results revealing nitrogen and oxygen functional groups in the GQDs-B. GQDs-B possess unique optical properties, rendering them suitable as fluorescence probes for ion detection of Fe3+ and Ce4+ ions, and the LOD is measured to be 0.0019 mM and 0.0025 mM, respectively. Notably, the Ce4+@GQDs-B probe shows particular promise in the detection of PO4 3-. This study presents a green, cost-effective and sustainable approach for the preparation of high-quality GQDs from biomass waste, offering extensive potential for practical applications in environmental sensing and bioanalysis.

Yingjuan Zhao: Project administration, Methodology. Meng Luo: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology. Siyu Yao: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Qianwei Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Wei Yu: Project administration, Methodology. Zufei Feng: Project administration, Methodology. Yinglin Yan: Project administration, Methodology. Yunhua Xu: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the Key Program funded by Science and Technology Department, Shaanxi Province (2024GX-YBXM-336), the Program of Shaanxi Province Innovation Capability Support-International Science and Technology Cooperation Base (2023GHJD-18), Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi Provincial Science and Technology Department (2024JC-YBQN-0152) and the Open Project of Shaanxi Energy Laboratory Basic Research Center (No. 2024-09).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.112363.
